Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict routine. I'd put in a lot of hours, like over 30 a week sometimes, but it was kind of scattered. I'm an auditory learner so I'd listen to lectures while doing other things, sometimes multiple times. It wasn't super organized but somehow I made it work, even if my assignment scores didn't always reflect the effort I was putting in.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. Some weeks I'd be really into it and study consistently, other weeks I'd let things slide until deadlines loomed. I think my anxiety made me swing between intense focus and just wanting to avoid everything. Deadlines were usually what snapped me back into action, even though I knew that wasn't the healthiest approach.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just this nagging anxiety that I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, even if I didn't actually do much once I was there. The fear of falling behind was a bigger motivator than genuine curiosity most of the time.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into the habit of checking the LMS pretty regularly, like almost daily. It became this thing I'd do first thing in the morning or late at night when I couldn't sleep because of anxiety. I wouldn't say it was enjoyable but it became automatic, like checking my phone for messages.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes just playing the audio while I was doing other stuff. The discussion posts from peers were helpful too when people actually explained concepts in different ways than the professor.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and PDFs most of the time. Reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. I'd download them thinking I'd get to them later but they'd just sit in a folder untouched. The discussion forums I barely posted in because honestly, typing out my thoughts felt like too much effort.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to doing well on exams. Since my exam scores were pretty good, I focused on what helped me understand concepts for tests. If something felt like busywork or wasn't going to be assessed, I'd deprioritize it, which probably explains my lower assignment scores.
Q8: Required activities got done because they had to, but I'd often rush through them without really engaging deeply. Optional stuff I almost never touched unless I was genuinely curious about something, which was rare. There's this weird pressure with required work that actually made me less engaged with it mentally, even though I completed it.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking around or lying in bed. I also used some YouTube videos and podcasts that explained similar topics in ways that clicked better for me. A lot of my actual learning happened through audio content that wasn't tracked anywhere.
Q10: There were tons of times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures or rewatching videos offline and really processing the material, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. Sometimes I'd have deep conversations with classmates about course concepts that helped me understand things better. None of that peer interaction showed up in the system even though it was valuable.
Q11: All the time. I'd stream a video once on the LMS and then download it or find it elsewhere to replay. I preferred having materials offline so I could access them without internet or when I was trying to avoid the anxiety of seeing all my other pending tasks on the platform. One view on the LMS often meant five listens offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Usually it was when assignments piled up and I felt overwhelmed by how much I'd let slide. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped me push through those moments, but it was still hard. Sometimes I'd just freeze up looking at everything I needed to do.
Q13: Fatigue hit me hard sometimes, especially when I was putting in those 30-plus hour weeks. When I was exhausted, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities weren't a huge issue since I don't do extracurriculars, but mental exhaustion from anxiety definitely affected how present I was when using the LMS.
Q14: Yeah, this happened a lot. I'd have the videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about whether I was going to mess up or fall behind. I'd technically watch the whole thing but couldn't tell you what was said. It was like my body was studying but my brain had checked out.
Q15: Assignments were my weak spot. I'd know they were important but something about starting them felt overwhelming, so I'd put them off. I think the anxiety made starting feel harder than it actually was. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later would become too late to do it well. It's frustrating because I knew better.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd cram a ton of activity into the days right before something was due. My LMS usage probably spiked dramatically around due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't ideal but the pressure of deadlines was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my avoidance patterns.
Q17: Feedback was kind of a mixed bag for me. Good feedback motivated me, but critical feedback on assignments sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid looking at it for days. Once I got over the initial dread, feedback did help me understand what to focus on. But that delay in processing it probably hurt my improvement on later work.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when motivated, bad because there wasn't enough external structure to keep me on track. Having seven online courses meant I was constantly juggling and sometimes this one got neglected despite my high engagement when I did focus on it.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. What made it harder was the volume of reading materials and the expectation to post in discussions, which just isn't my strength. Also, the assignment structure where things were worth a lot of points made me anxious about starting them, which paradoxically led to worse performance.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's genuinely trying to learn and engaging with materials but struggles with the actual submission part. The anxiety of turning something in and being judged can be paralyzing. You might spend hours watching videos and preparing but then freeze when it's time to actually produce something to submit.
Q21: That's kind of my story with exams. I didn't always show consistent LMS activity but I performed really well on tests because I was learning in ways that didn't show up in the logs. Listening to downloaded content, talking with peers in person, processing things in my head during walks. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show what's actually happening in your head. They miss offline studying, they miss the quality of attention you're giving something, and they definitely miss the emotional experience of learning. A lot of my real engagement happened in spaces the LMS couldn't see.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when the content sticks with you and you find yourself thinking about it even when you're not studying. It's not about how many times you log in or how many posts you make. It's about whether the learning is actually happening in your brain.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety influenced my patterns, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't capture the surprise I felt when I realized I could do well on exams despite my messy approach to assignments. And it definitely wouldn't show all those hours listening to lectures offline while trying to calm my racing thoughts.
